question.text: Scenario: Mr. Vikram Mehta, aged 55, has Rs. 50 lakh to invest in fixed income instruments. He is considering two options: Bond A — AAA-rated corporate bond: Face value Rs. 100, Coupon rate 9% (annual payments), Remaining maturity 5 years, Current market price Rs. 104.10, Credit rating AAA. Bond B — Government Security: Face value Rs. 100, Coupon rate 7.5% (semi-annual payments), Remaining maturity 10 years, Current market price Rs. 95.50, Issued by Government of India. Additional information: The current 5-year G-Sec yield is 7.2%. The credit spread for AAA-rated corporate bonds over G-Secs is typically 80-100 basis points. The day count convention for Indian bonds is 30/360. Vikram wants to understand the yield, duration risk, and reinvestment risk of each option before making a decision. He plans to hold the bonds until maturity. Question 4 of 5: Which bond carries higher interest rate risk (price sensitivity to yield changes), and why?

answer.text: Bond A, because it has a higher coupon rate
Bond B, because it has a longer maturity and lower coupon rate
Both have the same interest rate risk since both are fixed-rate bonds
Bond A, because it trades at a premium

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Bond B has higher interest rate risk due to two factors: (1) Longer maturity (10 years vs 5 years) — longer maturity means higher duration and greater price sensitivity, and (2) Lower coupon rate (7.5% vs 9%) — lower coupon means a larger proportion of cash flows come at maturity, further increasing duration. **The Trap:** Higher coupon actually REDUCES interest rate risk (shorter duration), not increases it. **Cross-Exam Context:** Duration and interest rate sensitivity drivers are tested in X-A and IV. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For a 55-year-old client planning to hold to maturity, Bond A with shorter duration is more appropriate — even though hold-to-maturity eliminates interest rate risk for principal, Mr. Mehta may need to liquidate early.
